title: DNA melting: intra base-pair dynamics and a vector generalization of the Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois model
abstract: The Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois (PBD) model of DNA denaturation, although successful in the description of melting profiles, fails to predict melting entropies, unzipping forces and dynamical properties, e.g. hairpin dynamics. The paper presents an atomistic "toy model" of the intra base-pair motion which suggests that the thermodynamics may be better described by a planar vector - rather than a scalar - order parameter. This leads to correct estimates of melting entropy, unzipping force, hairpin opening rates, and the equilibrium constant of open/closed base pair states during imino proton exchange.
summary: This paper proposes a new 'vector' version of the PBD model to better describe DNA melting. By treating base-pair separation as a 2D vector rather than a 1D scalar, the model accounts for the internal degrees of freedom within the base plane. This modification successfully corrects major discrepancies in predicted melting entropy and unzipping forces. Most significantly, it reduces the error in DNA hairpin opening rates from 17 orders of magnitude to a much more reasonable factor of 20.
main_problem: The original scalar Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois (PBD) model fails to accurately predict DNA melting entropies, unzipping forces, and DNA hairpin opening rates.
main_result: By generalizing the PBD model from a 1D scalar to a 2D vector order parameter, the model achieves much better agreement with experimental values for melting entropy, unzipping force, and hairpin kinetics.
method: The author uses an atomistic toy model to derive a vector-based Hamiltonian and employs the Transfer Integral (TI) operator method to calculate partition functions and thermodynamic quantities.
model_or_system: The system is DNA denaturation (melting) modeled as a chain of nonlinear oscillators. The new model treats each base pair as a planar vector with two independent 1D oscillators representing intra base-pair motion.
key_observables: Melting entropy, unzipping force, hairpin opening rates, melting fraction, and imino proton exchange equilibrium constants.
important_parameters: Morse potential depth (D) and width (1/alpha), stacking interaction parameters (b, k, rho), temperature (T), and salt concentration.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the dynamics are restricted to a planar geometry and that the mass of the base core is much larger than the attachment masses, allowing the core to be treated as fixed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the atomistic toy model of a single base plane; Figure 2 shows the melting entropy and fraction for a single PBD chain; Figure 4 shows thermodynamic integration results for AT and GC sequences.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the failure of the scalar PBD model, presents an atomistic toy model for a vector generalization, derives the new Hamiltonian, calculates thermodynamic and kinetic observables, and compares results to experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: A toy model of a single base plane in the DNA double helix. The large full circles reperesent the core of each base, of mass M and M ′, respectively, and are positioned at diametri- cally opposite points of a circle of radius R. Attached to each base at a distance l = 2R sin(π/8) are two point masses, one smaller, mimicking the carbonyl and exocyclic amino groups, and another approximating endocyclic nitrogen and its sur- rounding unit. The angle between the bond connecting the mass mi to its neighboring base core and the horizontal line is ϕi; at equilibrium, ϕ(0) 2 = ϕ(0) 1 −π = π/8. The dashed lines represent the effective couplings between the complementary bases; they are...

FIG. 2: Melting entropy for a single PBD chain. The onset shows that the curves for the entropy s1 (right y-axis, red, dashed) and the melting fraction (left y-axis, solid, blue) θ coincide when appropriately rescaled. The main plot shows the temperature derivative of the anharmonic part of the en- tropy vs the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (open circles). The linear fit to the data through the origin yields the slope ∆s∗. The absence of scattering in the data points (which originate in different regions of the melting pro- file) confirms the validity of the Ansatz (6) and, hence, that the melting entropy of a base pair is largely independent of chemical composition and/or...
FIG. 3: The eigenfunction ψ0 in the homogeneous case. Note the purely exponential asymptotics which allow the extraction of ξ⊥for GC and AT. Parameters are the same as in Section III.

FIG. 4: Extraction of single chain PBD (N=1000) average energies and entropies per site (cf Fig. 1); left panel: pure AT: ds/dT as a function of the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (squares, left y-axis); dϵ/dT as a function of the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (cir- cles, right y-axis); the lines represent least square fits and their slopes provide estimates for melting entropy and en- ergy, respectively; the onset shows the temperature derivative of the melting fraction as a function of temperature. right panel: same for pure GC. The Morse depth parameters used, DAT = 0.1291 eV , DGC = 0.1681, correspond to a salt con- centration 0.2M.
